Stochastic Differential Equations

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Gaussian Processes and Linear Dynamical Systems. It connects forward to Denoising Diffusion Models, where the reverse-time SDE is the basis of score-based generation, and to Markov Chain Monte Carlo, where Langevin dynamics exploits stationary distributions of SDEs.

Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) describe the continuous-time evolution of systems driven by random noise. They are the continuous-time counterpart of the linear dynamical systems studied in Part IV and underpin a broad range of models in statistics, physics, finance, and machine learning. In this course they arise most prominently in two places: as the principled mathematical framework for denoising diffusion models, and as a lens through which many stationary Gaussian processes can be formulated and computed with efficiently.

Brownian Motion¶

The fundamental source of randomness in continuous-time stochastic systems is Brownian motion (also called the Wiener process).

Definition: Brownian motion

A stochastic process {wt:t≥0}\{w_t : t \geq 0\}{wt​:t≥0} is a standard Brownian motion if:

  1. w0=0w_0 = 0w0​=0 almost surely.

  2. Independent increments: for 0≤s<t0 \leq s < t0≤s<t, the increment wt−wsw_t - w_swt​−ws​ is independent of {wr:r≤s}\{w_r : r \leq s\}{wr​:r≤s}.

  3. Gaussian increments: wt−ws∼N(0, t−s)w_t - w_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, t - s)wt​−ws​∼N(0,t−s).

  4. Continuous paths: t↦wtt \mapsto w_tt↦wt​ is continuous almost surely.

A ddd-dimensional Brownian motion wt∈Rd\mathbf{w}_t \in \mathbb{R}^dwt​∈Rd is a vector of ddd independent scalar Brownian motions, so wt−ws∼N(0, (t−s)I)\mathbf{w}_t - \mathbf{w}_s \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\, (t-s)I)wt​−ws​∼N(0,(t−s)I).

Brownian motion is nowhere differentiable almost surely: sample paths have unbounded variation on every interval and are far too rough for a classical derivative to exist. This is not a pathology — it is essential for the central limit theorem intuition underlying the construction. As we add finer and finer independent noise increments, the accumulated noise scales as t\sqrt{t}t​ (not ttt), and this Δt\sqrt{\Delta t}Δt​ scaling of increments is what forces us to treat Brownian motion differently from smooth driving signals.

The Ito Integral¶

Because wtw_twt​ is not differentiable, integrals of the form ∫0tσ(us,s) dws\int_0^t \sigma(u_s, s)\,dw_s∫0t​σ(us​,s)dws​ must be defined with care. The Ito integral constructs this as an L2L^2L2 limit using left-endpoint Riemann sums:

∫0tσ(us,s) dws  =  lim⁡n→∞∑i=0n−1σ(uti,ti) [wti+1−wti],ti=i tn.\int_0^t \sigma(u_s, s)\,dw_s \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sigma(u_{t_i}, t_i)\,\bigl[w_{t_{i+1}} - w_{t_i}\bigr], \qquad t_i = \tfrac{i\,t}{n}.∫0t​σ(us​,s)dws​=n→∞lim​i=0∑n−1​σ(uti​​,ti​)[wti+1​​−wti​​],ti​=nit​.
(1)

The integrand must be adapted to the Brownian filtration {Ft}\{\mathcal{F}_t\}{Ft​}, meaning σ(ut,t)\sigma(u_t, t)σ(ut​,t) may depend only on the history up to time ttt, not on future values of www.

Two key properties of the Ito integral follow directly from the construction.

Zero mean. Each summand σ(uti,ti)[wti+1−wti]\sigma(u_{t_i}, t_i)[w_{t_{i+1}} - w_{t_i}]σ(uti​​,ti​)[wti+1​​−wti​​] has zero conditional expectation given Fti\mathcal{F}_{t_i}Fti​​, because the Brownian increment is independent of the past and has mean zero. By the tower property, every partial sum has mean zero, and the limit inherits this:

E ⁣[∫0tσ(us,s) dws]=0.\mathbb{E}\!\left[\int_0^t \sigma(u_s, s)\,dw_s\right] = 0.E[∫0t​σ(us​,s)dws​]=0.
(2)

Ito isometry. Cross-terms in (∑iσtiΔwi)2\bigl(\sum_i \sigma_{t_i}\Delta w_i\bigr)^2(∑i​σti​​Δwi​)2 vanish for i≠ji \neq ji=j by the same independence argument. Only the diagonal terms contribute, using E[(Δwi)2]=Δt\mathbb{E}[(\Delta w_i)^2] = \Delta tE[(Δwi​)2]=Δt:

E ⁣[(∫0tσ(us,s) dws) ⁣2]=∫0tE ⁣[σ(us,s)2]ds.\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\int_0^t \sigma(u_s, s)\,dw_s\right)^{\!2}\right] = \int_0^t \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sigma(u_s, s)^2\right] ds.E[(∫0t​σ(us​,s)dws​)2]=∫0t​E[σ(us​,s)2]ds.
(3)

The isometry says that the L2L^2L2 norm of the integral equals the L2L^2L2 norm of the integrand in the product space [0,t]×Ω[0, t] \times \Omega[0,t]×Ω.

Stochastic Differential Equations¶

Starting from Euler’s method for the ODE u˙=f(u,t)\dot{u} = f(u, t)u˙=f(u,t),

u(t+Δt)=u(t)+f(u(t),t) Δt,u(t + \Delta t) = u(t) + f(u(t), t)\,\Delta t,u(t+Δt)=u(t)+f(u(t),t)Δt,
(4)

we augment each step with a Gaussian noise term whose variance scales as Δt\Delta tΔt — matching the variance of a Brownian increment over the same interval:

u(t+Δt)=u(t)+f(u(t),t) Δt+σ(u(t),t) εΔt,ε∼N(0,I).u(t + \Delta t) = u(t) + f(u(t), t)\,\Delta t + \sigma(u(t), t)\,\varepsilon\sqrt{\Delta t}, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I).u(t+Δt)=u(t)+f(u(t),t)Δt+σ(u(t),t)εΔt​,ε∼N(0,I).
(5)

Recognizing εΔt=w(t+Δt)−w(t)\varepsilon\sqrt{\Delta t} = w(t + \Delta t) - w(t)εΔt​=w(t+Δt)−w(t) and taking Δt→0\Delta t \to 0Δt→0 yields the stochastic differential equation

dut=f(ut,t) dt+σ(ut,t) dwt,du_t = f(u_t, t)\,dt + \sigma(u_t, t)\,dw_t,dut​=f(ut​,t)dt+σ(ut​,t)dwt​,
(6)

interpreted rigorously as the integral equation

ut=u0+∫0tf(us,s) ds+∫0tσ(us,s) dws.u_t = u_0 + \int_0^t f(u_s, s)\,ds + \int_0^t \sigma(u_s, s)\,dw_s.ut​=u0​+∫0t​f(us​,s)ds+∫0t​σ(us​,s)dws​.
(7)

The function f:Rd×R+→Rdf : \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}^df:Rd×R+​→Rd is the drift coefficient and σ:Rd×R+→Rd×d\sigma : \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}σ:Rd×R+​→Rd×d is the diffusion coefficient. The process {ut}\{u_t\}{ut​} solving the SDE is called an Ito process or diffusion process. Under standard Lipschitz and growth conditions on fff and σ\sigmaσ, existence and uniqueness of a strong solution are guaranteed.

Why Gaussian noise? Beyond analytical tractability, the central limit theorem gives physical justification: a large number of small independent perturbations accumulate into something Gaussian. The SDE framework can be generalized to other driving processes (any semimartingale), but Gaussian noise is by far the most common choice.

Ito’s Lemma¶

The ordinary chain rule says: if u(t)u(t)u(t) is a smooth function and ϕ\phiϕ is smooth, then dϕ(u)=ϕ′(u) dud\phi(u) = \phi'(u)\,dudϕ(u)=ϕ′(u)du. For an Ito process, there is an additional correction term arising from the quadratic variation of Brownian motion.

Theorem: Ito’s lemma

Let {ut}\{u_t\}{ut​} satisfy dut=ft dt+σt dwtdu_t = f_t\,dt + \sigma_t\,dw_tdut​=ft​dt+σt​dwt​ (scalar case) and let ϕ:R×R+→R\phi : \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}ϕ:R×R+​→R be twice continuously differentiable. Then

dϕ(ut,t)=(∂ϕ∂t+ft∂ϕ∂u+σt22∂2ϕ∂u2)dt+σt∂ϕ∂u dwt.d\phi(u_t, t) = \left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + f_t \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial u} + \frac{\sigma_t^2}{2} \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial u^2}\right)dt + \sigma_t \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial u}\,dw_t.dϕ(ut​,t)=(∂t∂ϕ​+ft​∂u∂ϕ​+2σt2​​∂u2∂2ϕ​)dt+σt​∂u∂ϕ​dwt​.
(8)

Derivation sketch. Expand ϕ\phiϕ to second order via Taylor’s theorem and substitute dut=ft dt+σt dwtdu_t = f_t\,dt + \sigma_t\,dw_tdut​=ft​dt+σt​dwt​:

dϕ=∂ϕ∂t dt+∂ϕ∂u dut+12∂2ϕ∂u2(dut)2+⋯d\phi = \frac{\partial\phi}{\partial t}\,dt + \frac{\partial\phi}{\partial u}\,du_t + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2\phi}{\partial u^2}(du_t)^2 + \cdotsdϕ=∂t∂ϕ​dt+∂u∂ϕ​dut​+21​∂u2∂2ϕ​(dut​)2+⋯
(9)

The key step is evaluating (dut)2=(ft dt+σt dwt)2(du_t)^2 = (f_t\,dt + \sigma_t\,dw_t)^2(dut​)2=(ft​dt+σt​dwt​)2. Using the multiplication rules dt2→0dt^2 \to 0dt2→0, dt dwt→0dt\,dw_t \to 0dtdwt​→0, and dwt2→dtdw_t^2 \to dtdwt2​→dt — which follow from the L2L^2L2 behavior of Brownian motion — gives (dut)2=σt2 dt(du_t)^2 = \sigma_t^2\,dt(dut​)2=σt2​dt. The higher-order terms vanish. The extra σt22∂2ϕ∂u2\frac{\sigma_t^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2\phi}{\partial u^2}2σt2​​∂u2∂2ϕ​ term, absent in ordinary calculus, is called the Ito correction.

Multivariate form. For ut∈Rd\mathbf{u}_t \in \mathbb{R}^dut​∈Rd satisfying dut=ft dt+Gt dwtd\mathbf{u}_t = \mathbf{f}_t\,dt + G_t\,d\mathbf{w}_tdut​=ft​dt+Gt​dwt​ and a smooth function ϕ:Rd×R+→R\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}ϕ:Rd×R+​→R,

dϕ=∂ϕ∂t dt+(∇uϕ)⊤dut+12tr⁡ ⁣(GtGt⊤∇u2ϕ)dt.d\phi = \frac{\partial\phi}{\partial t}\,dt + (\nabla_u \phi)^\top d\mathbf{u}_t + \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}\!\left(G_t G_t^\top \nabla_u^2 \phi\right)dt.dϕ=∂t∂ϕ​dt+(∇u​ϕ)⊤dut​+21​tr(Gt​Gt⊤​∇u2​ϕ)dt.
(10)

Example: geometric Brownian motion. The SDE dut=μut dt+σut dwtdu_t = \mu u_t\,dt + \sigma u_t\,dw_tdut​=μut​dt+σut​dwt​ is the classical model for asset prices. Applying Ito’s lemma to ϕ(u)=log⁡u\phi(u) = \log uϕ(u)=logu gives

dlog⁡ut=(μ−σ22)dt+σ dwt,d\log u_t = \left(\mu - \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)dt + \sigma\,dw_t,dlogut​=(μ−2σ2​)dt+σdwt​,
(11)

so log⁡ut\log u_tlogut​ drifts linearly and ut=u0exp⁡((μ−σ22)t+σwt)u_t = u_0\exp\bigl((\mu - \frac{\sigma^2}{2})t + \sigma w_t\bigr)ut​=u0​exp((μ−2σ2​)t+σwt​) is log-normally distributed. The −σ2/2-\sigma^2/2−σ2/2 correction relative to the drift μ\muμ is a direct consequence of the Ito term.

The Fokker-Planck Equation¶

Instead of tracking individual trajectories, one can describe the evolution of the marginal density pt(u)p_t(u)pt​(u) of the process. For the SDE dut=f(ut,t) dt+σ(ut,t) dwtdu_t = f(u_t, t)\,dt + \sigma(u_t, t)\,dw_tdut​=f(ut​,t)dt+σ(ut​,t)dwt​, the marginal density satisfies the Fokker-Planck equation (also called the forward Kolmogorov equation):

∂pt∂t(u)=−∇u⋅[f(u,t) pt(u)]+12 ∇u2 ⁣:[σ(u,t)σ(u,t)⊤pt(u)].\frac{\partial p_t}{\partial t}(u) = -\nabla_u \cdot \bigl[f(u, t)\,p_t(u)\bigr] + \frac{1}{2}\,\nabla_u^2\!:\bigl[\sigma(u,t)\sigma(u,t)^\top p_t(u)\bigr].∂t∂pt​​(u)=−∇u​⋅[f(u,t)pt​(u)]+21​∇u2​:[σ(u,t)σ(u,t)⊤pt​(u)].
(12)

In the scalar case with state-independent diffusion σ(t)\sigma(t)σ(t), this simplifies to

∂pt∂t=−∂∂u ⁣[f(u,t) pt]+σ(t)22∂2pt∂u2.\frac{\partial p_t}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial u}\!\bigl[f(u,t)\,p_t\bigr] + \frac{\sigma(t)^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2 p_t}{\partial u^2}.∂t∂pt​​=−∂u∂​[f(u,t)pt​]+2σ(t)2​∂u2∂2pt​​.
(13)

The first term describes probability transport due to the drift — it has the form of a continuity equation. The second term is a diffusion equation that spreads probability mass. Together they give a precise PDE governing how the distribution of utu_tut​ evolves over time.

The backward Kolmogorov equation is the adjoint of the Fokker-Planck operator and describes how expected functions of the terminal state E[ϕ(uT)∣ut=u]\mathbb{E}[\phi(u_T) \mid u_t = u]E[ϕ(uT​)∣ut​=u] evolve backward in time from TTT to ttt. It is the continuous-time analogue of the backwards recursions in dynamic programming.

Simulation: Euler–Maruyama¶

Given initial condition u0∼P0u_0 \sim P_0u0​∼P0​ and a time grid 0=t0<t1<⋯<tn=T0 = t_0 < t_1 < \cdots < t_n = T0=t0​<t1​<⋯<tn​=T with step Δt\Delta tΔt, the Euler–Maruyama method approximates the SDE solution by

uti+1=uti+f(uti,ti) Δt+σ(uti,ti) εiΔt,εi∼iidN(0,I).u_{t_{i+1}} = u_{t_i} + f(u_{t_i}, t_i)\,\Delta t + \sigma(u_{t_i}, t_i)\,\varepsilon_i\sqrt{\Delta t}, \qquad \varepsilon_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, I).uti+1​​=uti​​+f(uti​​,ti​)Δt+σ(uti​​,ti​)εi​Δt​,εi​∼iidN(0,I).
(14)

This is the direct stochastic analogue of Euler’s method and has strong convergence rate O(Δt)O(\sqrt{\Delta t})O(Δt​). The Milstein scheme adds a correction using the Ito lemma applied to σ\sigmaσ,

uti+1=uti+f Δt+σ εiΔt+12σσ′((εi)2−1)Δt,u_{t_{i+1}} = u_{t_i} + f\,\Delta t + \sigma\,\varepsilon_i\sqrt{\Delta t} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma\sigma'\bigl((\varepsilon_i)^2 - 1\bigr)\Delta t,uti+1​​=uti​​+fΔt+σεi​Δt​+21​σσ′((εi​)2−1)Δt,
(15)

improving the strong convergence rate to O(Δt)O(\Delta t)O(Δt) when σ\sigmaσ depends on the state. When the transition density pti,ti+1(uti+1∣uti)p_{t_i, t_{i+1}}(u_{t_{i+1}} \mid u_{t_i})pti​,ti+1​​(uti+1​​∣uti​​) is known in closed form — as it is for linear SDEs — exact simulation is possible without any discretization error.

Linear SDEs and the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck Process¶

The most tractable family of SDEs has a drift that is linear in the state:

dut=(A(t)ut+b(t))dt+G(t) dwt.d\mathbf{u}_t = \bigl(A(t)\mathbf{u}_t + \mathbf{b}(t)\bigr)dt + G(t)\,d\mathbf{w}_t.dut​=(A(t)ut​+b(t))dt+G(t)dwt​.
(16)

Because the noise enters linearly, solutions have Gaussian transition densities and therefore define Gaussian processes.

Solution via integrating factor. For the scalar time-invariant case dut=a ut dt+g dwtdu_t = a\,u_t\,dt + g\,dw_tdut​=aut​dt+gdwt​, apply Ito’s lemma to ϕ(ut,t)=e−atut\phi(u_t, t) = e^{-at}u_tϕ(ut​,t)=e−atut​:

d(e−atut)=e−at g dwt.d(e^{-at}u_t) = e^{-at}\,g\,dw_t.d(e−atut​)=e−atgdwt​.
(17)

Integrating both sides from 0 to ttt and rearranging,

ut=eatu0+g∫0tea(t−s) dws.u_t = e^{at}u_0 + g\int_0^t e^{a(t-s)}\,dw_s.ut​=eatu0​+g∫0t​ea(t−s)dws​.
(18)

The Ito integral is Gaussian (as an L2L^2L2 limit of Gaussian sums), so the transition density is

p(ut∣u0)=N ⁣(eatu0,    g2∫0te2a(t−s) ds).p(u_t \mid u_0) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(e^{at}u_0,\;\; g^2\int_0^t e^{2a(t-s)}\,ds\right).p(ut​∣u0​)=N(eatu0​,g2∫0t​e2a(t−s)ds).
(19)

For a<0a < 0a<0, the mean decays exponentially toward zero and the variance saturates at g2/(2∣a∣)g^2/(2|a|)g2/(2∣a∣) as t→∞t \to \inftyt→∞.

The Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process. The canonical mean-reverting SDE is

dut=−θ(ut−μ) dt+σ dwt,θ>0.du_t = -\theta(u_t - \mu)\,dt + \sigma\,dw_t, \qquad \theta > 0.dut​=−θ(ut​−μ)dt+σdwt​,θ>0.
(20)

The drift pulls utu_tut​ toward the long-run mean μ\muμ at rate θ\thetaθ; σ\sigmaσ controls the noise level. The transition density is

p(ut∣us)=N ⁣(μ+e−θ(t−s)(us−μ),    σ22θ(1−e−2θ(t−s))).p(u_t \mid u_s) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu + e^{-\theta(t-s)}(u_s - \mu),\;\; \frac{\sigma^2}{2\theta}\bigl(1 - e^{-2\theta(t-s)}\bigr)\right).p(ut​∣us​)=N(μ+e−θ(t−s)(us​−μ),2θσ2​(1−e−2θ(t−s))).
(21)

The marginal distribution converges to the stationary distribution N(μ, σ2/(2θ))\mathcal{N}(\mu,\, \sigma^2/(2\theta))N(μ,σ2/(2θ)) regardless of the initial condition, with the exponential covariance kernel k(s,t)=σ22θe−θ∣t−s∣k(s, t) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\theta}e^{-\theta|t-s|}k(s,t)=2θσ2​e−θ∣t−s∣.

Multivariate case. For dut=Aut dt+G dwtd\mathbf{u}_t = A\mathbf{u}_t\,dt + G\,d\mathbf{w}_tdut​=Aut​dt+Gdwt​ with stable AAA, the solution is

ut=eAtu0+∫0teA(t−s)G dws,\mathbf{u}_t = e^{At}\mathbf{u}_0 + \int_0^t e^{A(t-s)}G\,d\mathbf{w}_s,ut​=eAtu0​+∫0t​eA(t−s)Gdws​,
(22)

with transition covariance P(t)=∫0teAsGG⊤eA⊤s dsP(t) = \int_0^t e^{As}GG^\top e^{A^\top s}\,dsP(t)=∫0t​eAsGG⊤eA⊤sds. The stationary covariance P∞P_\inftyP∞​ is the unique positive definite solution to the continuous-time Lyapunov equation

AP∞+P∞A⊤+GG⊤=0.AP_\infty + P_\infty A^\top + GG^\top = 0.AP∞​+P∞​A⊤+GG⊤=0.
(23)

Stationary Distributions¶

When does the density ptp_tpt​ converge as t→∞t \to \inftyt→∞? A stationary distribution p∗p^*p∗ satisfies the time-independent Fokker-Planck equation (right-hand side set to zero). For the scalar constant-diffusion case σ(u,t)=σ\sigma(u, t) = \sigmaσ(u,t)=σ, one can integrate the Fokker-Planck equation twice to obtain

p∗(u)∝exp⁡ ⁣(2σ2∫uf(v) dv).p^*(u) \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{2}{\sigma^2}\int^u f(v)\,dv\right).p∗(u)∝exp(σ22​∫uf(v)dv).
(24)

This is the Boltzmann distribution with energy E(u)=−∫uf(v) dvE(u) = -\int^u f(v)\,dvE(u)=−∫uf(v)dv.

Langevin dynamics. This connection between drift and stationary distribution is the foundation of gradient-based MCMC. To sample from a target distribution p∗(u)∝e−E(u)p^*(u) \propto e^{-E(u)}p∗(u)∝e−E(u), one runs the SDE

dut=−∇E(ut) dt+2 dwt,du_t = -\nabla E(u_t)\,dt + \sqrt{2}\,dw_t,dut​=−∇E(ut​)dt+2​dwt​,
(25)

which has p∗(u)∝e−E(u)p^*(u) \propto e^{-E(u)}p∗(u)∝e−E(u) as its unique stationary distribution. In the Bayesian context, E(θ)=−log⁡p(x∣θ)−log⁡p(θ)E(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) - \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta})E(θ)=−logp(x∣θ)−logp(θ), and Langevin dynamics provides a continuous-time limit of gradient-based MCMC. Discretization via Euler–Maruyama gives the unadjusted Langevin algorithm (ULA); adding a Metropolis–Hastings correction step recovers the Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA).

Linear SDEs as Gaussian Processes¶

Since solutions to linear SDEs have Gaussian finite-dimensional distributions, they are Gaussian processes. The GP mean and covariance can be computed from the SDE coefficients:

m(t)=E[ut],k(s,t)=Cov⁡(us,ut),m(t) = \mathbb{E}[u_t], \qquad k(s, t) = \operatorname{Cov}(u_s, u_t),m(t)=E[ut​],k(s,t)=Cov(us​,ut​),
(26)

both of which satisfy ODEs derivable from the SDE.

Conversely, many classical stationary covariance kernels correspond exactly to the stationary GPs generated by specific linear SDEs:

KernelCorresponding SDE
Exponential: $k(r) = \sigma^2 e^{-\ellr
Matérn-3/2: k(r)=(1+3ℓr)e−3ℓrk(r) = (1 + \sqrt{3}\ell r)e^{-\sqrt{3}\ell r}k(r)=(1+3​ℓr)e−3​ℓrSecond-order linear SDE (two-state system)
Matérn-5/2: k(r)=(1+5ℓr+53ℓ2r2)e−5ℓrk(r) = (1 + \sqrt{5}\ell r + \tfrac{5}{3}\ell^2 r^2)e^{-\sqrt{5}\ell r}k(r)=(1+5​ℓr+35​ℓ2r2)e−5​ℓrThird-order linear SDE (three-state system)
Squared exponential (approximate)Infinite-order SDE, approximated by truncating a series expansion

This SDE–GP equivalence has an important computational consequence. Standard GP regression with NNN observations requires O(N3)O(N^3)O(N3) time to compute (and invert) the N×NN \times NN×N covariance matrix. When the covariance function corresponds to a linear SDE, the Kalman filter performs the same inference in O(N)O(N)O(N) time, by exploiting the Markov structure of the SDE solution. This makes SDE-based GP approximation a key technique for large-scale time series Solin & Särkkä, 2020.

The Reverse-Time SDE¶

A foundational result due to Anderson, 1982 shows that the time-reverse of a diffusion process is also a diffusion. If {ut:t∈[0,T]}\{u_t : t \in [0, T]\}{ut​:t∈[0,T]} satisfies the forward SDE

dut=f(ut,t) dt+σ(t) dwt,du_t = f(u_t, t)\,dt + \sigma(t)\,dw_t,dut​=f(ut​,t)dt+σ(t)dwt​,
(27)

then the reverse-time process uˉt=uT−t\bar{u}_t = u_{T-t}uˉt​=uT−t​ satisfies

duˉt=[−f(uˉt,T−t)+σ(T−t)2 ∇ulog⁡pT−t(uˉt)]dt+σ(T−t) dwˉt,d\bar{u}_t = \bigl[-f(\bar{u}_t, T-t) + \sigma(T-t)^2\,\nabla_u \log p_{T-t}(\bar{u}_t)\bigr]dt + \sigma(T-t)\,d\bar{w}_t,duˉt​=[−f(uˉt​,T−t)+σ(T−t)2∇u​logpT−t​(uˉt​)]dt+σ(T−t)dwˉt​,
(28)

where wˉt\bar{w}_twˉt​ is a new Brownian motion (running backward) and ∇ulog⁡pt(u)\nabla_u \log p_t(u)∇u​logpt​(u) is the score function of the marginal density at time ttt.

The reverse SDE has the same marginal distributions as the forward SDE, but run in reverse time. Starting from uˉ0∼pT\bar{u}_0 \sim p_Tuˉ0​∼pT​ (a simple noise distribution) and simulating the reverse SDE to time TTT produces a sample from p0p_0p0​ (the data distribution).

This result is the mathematical heart of score-based generative modeling: design a forward SDE that converts data into noise, estimate the score ∇ulog⁡pt\nabla_u \log p_t∇u​logpt​ using a neural network trained by denoising score matching, then run the reverse SDE to generate new data. The connection between the reverse-time correction and the score function explains why learning to denoise is equivalent to learning to generate — a remarkable duality discussed further in the Denoising Diffusion Models chapter.

Conclusion¶

Stochastic differential equations extend ordinary differential equations by adding Brownian noise, yielding continuous-time models whose sample paths are nowhere differentiable. The Ito integral and Ito’s lemma provide the calculus needed to work with these processes. Linear SDEs are especially tractable: their solutions are Gaussian processes with kernels that correspond to classical covariance functions (exponential, Matérn), and the Kalman filter performs GP inference in linear time by exploiting the Markov structure. Stationary distributions of SDEs are Boltzmann densities, connecting SDEs to Langevin MCMC. The reverse-time SDE of Anderson (1982) is the mathematical foundation of score-based generative modeling, providing a principled framework for understanding denoising diffusion models.

Next Steps

This chapter completes Part V. The two most direct applications of SDEs developed here are:

  • Denoising Diffusion Models — the reverse-time SDE underpins score-based generation; this chapter provides its mathematical foundation

References¶
  1. Solin, A., & Särkkä, S. (2020). Hilbert space methods for reduced-rank Gaussian process regression. Statistics and Computing, 30(2), 419–446.
  2. Anderson, B. D. O. (1982). Reverse-time diffusion equation models. Stochastic Processes and Their Applications, 12(3), 313–326.
